coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal regulation of fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots derived from biomass for zebrafish imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yao
Tang, Yaoyao
Xu, Quan
Cao, Yi
Wei, Jianfei
Liang, Zhiqiang
Jiang, Xinyi
Wu, Ying
Guo, Chaozhong
coredata.dc:description: The high-value-added exploitation of biomass is a critical way to supply new carbon nanomaterials for “green resources."Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as highly promising fluorescent nanomaterials. Carbon dots (FS-CDs) were prepared in this study using a simple solvothermal process, with fish scales serving as the carbon source. The FS-CDs were found to be evenly distributed, displaying an average diameter of 2.52 nm. Additionally, they demonstrated excitation-dependent emission and exhibited a notably high quantum yield of 31.71%. The high photoluminescence quantum yield in FS-CDs was shown to be associated with the (N, O)-codoped and high content of doped nitrogen, as determined by spectroscopic and microscopic examination in conjunction with the theoretical lattice model. Furthermore, it was discovered that FS-CDs exhibit a notable capacity for fluorescence imaging in zebrafish. This study demonstrates the significant utilization value of fish scales, which expands their potential applications and offers an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and scalable approach for producing biomass-derived CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fish scale
Biomass-based carbon dots
Heteroatom doped
Photoluminescence quantum yield
Fluorescence probe
Zebrafish imaging

Theoretical exploration of the PLQY enhancement mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (KJQN202001335 and KJQN202101313), Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQJQX0032), National Defense Science and Technology Excellence Youth Fund 2022-JCJQ-ZQ-018.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic depiction of the synthesis of FS-CDs. (b) PL emission spectra of FS-CDs under different excitation light (ex:320∼540 nm). (c) The PLQY of FS-CDs. (d) UV–vis spectra of FS-CDs. (e) Fluorescent lifetime decay curve of FS-CDs.
(a) TEM images of FS-CDs. (b) The diameter distribution of FS-CDs was computed utilizing the TEM image. (c–d) The HRTEM images of the FS-CDs.
(a) Raman spectrum of FS-CDs powder. (b) FT-IR spectrum of the aqueous FS-CDs solution. (c) The XPS spectrum of the FS-CDs. (d–f) High-resolution XPS C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra for FS-CDs.
The total density of states (TDOS) for N- doped CDs (a), O-doped CDs (b), and (N, O)-codoped CDs (c). The responding PDOS are presented as (a′), (b′), and (c′).
(a) The images of negative control zebrafish larvae, (b). Green fluorescence images of entire zebrafish bodies after injection of FS-CD solution (diluted 100 times) for 24 h.
Comparison of FS-CDs Synthesized in this work together with other previously reported FS-CDs.
Comparison between known CDs and FS-CDs prepared in the present investigation.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal regulation of fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots derived from biomass for zebrafish imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yao Liu and Yaoyao Tang contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high-value-added exploitation of biomass is a critical way to supply new carbon nanomaterials for “green resources."Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as highly promising fluorescent nanomaterials. Carbon dots (FS-CDs) were prepared in this study using a simple solvothermal process, with fish scales serving as the carbon source. The FS-CDs were found to be evenly distributed, displaying an average diameter of 2.52 nm. Additionally, they demonstrated excitation-dependent emission and exhibited a notably high quantum yield of 31.71%. The high photoluminescence quantum yield in FS-CDs was shown to be associated with the (N, O)-codoped and high content of doped nitrogen, as determined by spectroscopic and microscopic examination in conjunction with the theoretical lattice model. Furthermore, it was discovered that FS-CDs exhibit a notable capacity for fluorescence imaging in zebrafish. This study demonstrates the significant utilization value of fish scales, which expands their potential applications and offers an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and scalable approach for producing biomass-derived CDs.

This study presents a method for producing biomass-based carbon dots from fish scales (FS-CDs) with a high quantum yield of 31.71%. According to a thorough investigation of chemical architecture, surface chemistry, and density functional theory, the high concentration of doped nitrogen and (N, O)-codoped nitrogen is most likely to be responsible for the high photoluminescence quantum yield in FS-CDs. Zebrafish fluorescent bioimaging was performed utilizing the FS-CDs sample as a probe. These findings support the use of FS-CDs as a probe in bioimaging studies. The findings of this study not only indicate the significant value-added characteristics of fish scales and their expanded potential applications, but also present an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and scalable approach for producing biomass-derived CDs.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs, or carbon dots, are a type of carbon-based nanomaterial that exhibit zero-dimensional characteristics. These nanomaterials possess diameters that are smaller than 10 nm and are primarily composed of carbon skeletons. CDs exhibit remarkable photochemical and electrochemical characteristics, along with biocompatibility and minimal toxicity. Consequently, they play a pivotal role in various domains such as lean energy, biomedicine, and environmental monitoring [1–6]. The extensive range of reaction precursors and the ease with which functionalization can be achieved have generated significant research attention towards CDs. In recent years, due to global warming and environmental pollution, an increasing number of “green resources” derived from organic biowaste with delicate structures, such as plant leaves, grass, food waste, coffee grounds, and cyanobacteria, are recycled and used to manufacture novel advanced carbon materials [7–13]. Recently, Lu et al. showed that garlic may be used to induce self-crosslinking in CDs (SC-CDs), which is then used to create the Ru/carbon-based electrocatalyst (Ru@SC-CDs) [14]. Shan et al. prepared multicolor biomass-based CDs using natural biomass sources, namely honeysuckle, turmeric, and perilla, as precursors for bacterial imaging [15]. Yang et al. prepared highly crystalline CDs utilizing ginkgo leaves as the starting material and a simple treatment [16]. However, the biomass-derived CDs have been reported to always have low Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and they are the unclear structure-performance relationship and luminescence mechanism, which hinders the application of biomass-based carbon dots in the field of bioimaging. Target to these problems, here we report a new approach towards the realization of biomass-based carbon dots from fish scales (FS-CDs) with a high quantum yield. Besides, the relationship between composition, structure, electron distribution state and photoluminescence performance of FS-CDs is revealed via structural characterization and performance evaluation. The PLQY enhancement mechanism is further investigated by theoretical calculations. This research lays the foundation for the development of high PLQY biomass-based carbon dots and the study of the PL mechanism of CDs. The findings of this study not only indicate the significant value-added characteristics of fish scales and their expanded potential applications, but also present an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and scalable approach for producing biomass-derived CDs.

In comparison to biomass sources that have been previously documented, fish scale (FS) biowaste exhibits a notable abundance of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and various mineral elements. These constituents are recognized as environmentally friendly and sustainable resources, making them suitable for the production of advanced functional carbon nanomaterials [17–19]. The collagen protein and hydroxyapatite contained in fish scales make positive contribute to the fluorescence enhancement [20].

In addition, FS is extremely eco-friendly and productive in terms of annual yield. Due to the aforementioned benefits, FS biowaste was selected as the raw material for the simple, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly synthesis of water-soluble FS-CDs via one-pot solvothermal treatment. The solvothermal treatment process facilitates the simultaneous occurrence of carbonization, surface functionalization, and nitrogen doping, resulting in the formation of carbon quantum dots exhibiting a significantly high fluorescence quantum yield of up to 31.71%. The emission peak of FS-CDs was determined to be 510 nm when excited at 440 nm, indicating that the emission of FS-CDs is dependent upon the excitation wavelength. Moreover, FS-CDs with low toxicity were demonstrated to be effective fluorescent probes for zebrafish imaging. Compared with previously reported FS-CDs with lower PLQY (about 3∼20%) via hydrothermal synthesis (Table 1 ), in this work, the pretreated FS biowaste was subjected to heating in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) solvents. DMF can be used to dehydrate and carbonize raw materials, which in turn promotes the development of a crystalline core. In the meantime, DMF as nitrogen source can give additional nitrogen element doping in CDs, which is accountable for the high quantum yield (over 30%). In addition, the PLQY enhancement mechanism are further investigated by theoretical calculations. This research lays the foundation for the development of high PLQY biomass-based carbon dots and the study of the PL mechanism of CDs.

N, N-dimethylformamide was purchased Chengdu Colon Chemical Co., LTD. Fresh scales of carp obtained from from Yonghui Supermarket. Adult zebrafish (wild-type AB strain, 4 months old, length 3.87 ± 0.33 cm, body weight 0.65 ± 0.05 g) were purchased from the Chinese Zebrafish Resource Center. Deionized water was produced by BK-10B from Dongguanshi Qianjing environmental equipment Co., Ltd.

Firstly, fish scales (obtained from fresh carp) were washed with deionized water and then dried at room temperature. Subsequently, FS were fragmented by the food processor. The treated samples were soaked in 0.5 M Na2CO3 and decalcified with 0.6 M HCl. After washing with deionized water, the FS were dried in a hood at room temperature. Next, 1 g of FS were dispersed in 20 ml DMF, and then the mixture was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The mixture was filtered using a cylindrical filtration membrane filter (0.22 mm) to obtain the final FS-CDs. After purification, the solution was concentrated using rotary evaporation to get solid FS-CDs.

The morphology of FS-CDs was characterized using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) from Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Ltd (Model JEM-2100 F). The photoluminescence (PL), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis), absolute fluorescence quantum yields, and life curves were measured using a multipurpose fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5) from Techcomp (China) Ltd. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was measured by a Bruker Vertex 70 V. Raman spectrum derived from the Horiba Jobin Yvon Xplora confocal Raman microscope at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical exploration of the PLQY enhancement mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: We propose a theoretical lattice model for N and O-doped carbon dots (CDs) to further understand the doping process and its impact on CDs. In this study, we used first-principles calculations to investigate the elemental states of doped atoms in single N or O atoms and co-doped N and O CDs, and studied the structure and electronic properties of CDs. For DFT calculations, we constructed a model with approximately 60 carbon atoms and a size of about 1.2 nm, considering O or N atoms at substitution sites. All calculations were performed using the VASP simulation software package, which uses plane wave basis sets and periodic boundary conditions, and employs the Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient-corrected approximation (GGA) functional. The Kohn-Sham equation was solved using the projector augmented wave (PAW) method, and the differential charge density, density of states (DOSs), and band structure of CDs were calculated to reveal the effects of doping impurity elements on CD properties.

In this work, adult zebrafish (wild-type AB strain, 4 months old, length 3.87 ± 0.33 cm, body weight 0.65 ± 0.05 g) were purchased from the Chinese Zebrafish Resource Center. The fishes were fed with freshly hatched live brine shrimp (Artemia salina) three times a day and raised at the temperature of 28 ± 1 °C under visible light for 14 h and dim light for 10 h. For crossing, groups of adult fishes (2 males and 1 female) were placed into the crossing tanks prefilled with enough water. After fertilization, the embryos were collected and hatched. Then, the seeds or zebrafish larvae were incubated with CDs for 24 h, and the green fluorescence was observed under a NIKON fluorescence microscope.

The TEM was employed to analyze the particle size and morphologies of the FS-CDs. The FS-CDs exhibited a spherical shape, were evenly distributed, and had an average diameter of 2.52 nm, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a and b). High-resolution TEM imaging indicated that the lattice distances of FS-CDs were 0.21 nm (Fig. 2(c and d)), corresponding to the (100) graphite carbon lattice planes. The disordered structure and defect of the FS-CDs were assessed using the Raman spectrum. Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the presence of two prominent peaks. The D band, located at 1336 cm−1,signifies the structural disorder within the sp2-domain. On the other hand, the G band, observed at 1550 cm−1, indicates the presence of the graphite phase. To assess the defect and graphite levels of the FS-CDs structure, anintensity ratio of the D band to the G band (I
D
/I
G ) was calculated. The calculated I
D
/I
G ratio of 0.395 suggests the presence of structural defects in the FS-CDs, which may contribute to the observed high quantum yield of FS-CDs.

The investigation of the structure and functional groups of the FS-CDs was conducted through the analysis of FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3(b)). The absorption at 1330 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of FS-CDs was attributed to dangling C–N bonds. The absorption peaks observed at 1457 and 1542 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO bond and the CC or C–N bonds, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of FS-CDs displayed a distinct peak at 1660 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO bond. Broad absorption at 2935 cm−1 was caused by C–H stretching vibrations, and the strong absorption at 3200 cm−1 was associated with the N–H stretching vibration. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to elucidate the chemical composition of the FS-CDs in greater detail. The XPS spectra indicated the contents of C (56.37%), N (24.37%), and O (19.26%) elements in FS-CDs (Fig. 3(c)). The chemical state of atoms inside FS-CDs was investigated using high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS peaks (Fig. 3(d–f)). Three peaks, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N, and CO, respectively, were identified in the C1s spectrum of FS-CDs with energies of 284.6, 285.4, and 288.7 eV. The N1s band observed in the FS-CDs exhibits two distinct peaks at 399.7 and 400.3 eV, which have been attributed to pyrrolic N and amine groups, respectively. The spectrum of O1s exhibited two distinct peaks at 532.2 and 531.5 eV, which can be attributed to the presence of C–O and CO bonds, respectively. Thus, the XPS spectraindicated that a graphite carbon core was formed during the hydrothermal process and that the FS-CDs contained functional groups, which was consistent with the FTIR. Element doping is widely recognized as a fundamental strategy for enhancing the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs [36–38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical exploration of the PLQY enhancement mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper comprehension of the nitrogen and oxygen heteroatom doping procedure and the underlying mechanism responsible for the enhancement of PLQY on CDs, three theoretical lattice models were proposed. These models encompass the N mono-doped CDs, O mono-doped CDs, and (N, O)-codoped CDs. First-principle calculations were performed to investigate the elemental states of doping atoms in spherical (N, O)-codoped CDs [37,39]. For density functional theory (DFT) calculations, a model with approximately 60 carbon atoms and a dimension of approximately 1.2 nm was constructed, with O.

or N atoms at substitution sites (Fig. 4 a–c, insets). The VASP simulation software package was utilized for all calculations. This software employed plane wave basis sets and periodic boundary conditions and incorporated the Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient-corrected approximation (GGA) functional. The Kohn-Sham equation was successfully solved utilizing the projector augmented wave (PAW) method. Subsequently, the differential charge density, density of states (DOSs), and band structure of CDs were computed to elucidate the impact of doping impurity elements on the properties of CDs [40]. For the N-, O-, and (N, O)-codoped CDs, Fig. 4 depicts the total and partial DOSs. The peaks associated with (N, O)-codoped CDs shift toward the HOMO in comparison to those of N-CDs and O-CDs. The observed phenomenon can be attributed to the co-doping of N and O, as indicated by the simulation results of the partial density of states (PDOS) shown in Fig. 4(a'-c'). Effective transport of electrons from the CDs' surface is aided by the energy gradient provided by the dangling N and N–O bonds. In (N, O)-codoped CDs, orbital hybridization and splitting peaks were observed owing to the heteroatom doping with nitrogen and oxygen. The DOSs intensity of (N, O)-codoped CDs was greater than that of N-CDs and O-CDs, indicating that the co-doping of N and O increases the electrical conductivity of the system. The aforementioned findings have clearly illustrated that the placement of the impurity atom significantly influences the control of the structural and electronic characteristics of doped CDs. The distribution of electron density difference (EDD) for (N, O)-codoped CDs was computed and is depicted in Fig. S8. The electron transfer between bonding atoms was directly manifested by the EDD. The isosurfaces in blue and yellow corresponded to electron depletion and accumulation, respectively, during the process of chemical bond formation. The oxygen and nitrogen sites observed in the plot exhibited a vivid yellow color, which can be attributed to their significantly higher electronegativity compared to other elements present in the (N, O)-codoped CDs. Therefore, the stable fluorescence properties and high PLQY displayed by doped CDs were proven to be the result of the shift of energy states in (N, O)-codoped CDs through an extensive spectroscopic and microscopic investigation in combination with the theoretical lattice model.

To investigate the sites of action on FS-CDs that result in high fluorescence quantum yields, a comparison was made between FS-CDs-DMF and FS-CDs-H2O, which were synthesized in an aqueous solution. The results indicated that the PLQY of FS-CDs-H2O was found to be 6.24% (Figs. S9–10). XPS was employed to elucidate the chemical structure of the FS-CDs-H2O (Fig. S11). The overall N content in FS-CDs-H2O was found to be 14.79%, whereas in FS-CDs-DMF it was determined to be 24.37%. Based on these findings, it is clear that DMF, a high-boiling nonaqueous solvent, can be used to dehydrate and carbonize raw materials, which in turn promotes the development of a crystalline core. In the meantime, DMF as nitrogen source can give additional nitrogen element doping in CDs, which is accountable for the high quantum yield [41].

The utilization of zebrafish, which possess favorable translucency and exhibit accelerated maturation, has been extensively employed in the field of bioimaging research [42]. To assess the imaging capacity of the FS-CDs, juvenile zebrafish were used for in vivo imaging and tissue distribution analysis. In this study, zebrafish larvae were subjected to a 24 h incubation period with CDs. No zebrafish deaths or deformities were observed. Subsequently, green fluorescence was observed using a NIKON fluorescence microscope (Fig. 5 (a)). The imaging findings of the zebrafish demonstrated that FS-CDs effectively stained the larvae of zebrafish. The negative control zebrafish larvae showed weak green autofluorescence, whereas FS-CD-exposed zebrafish larvae were stained with green fluorescence in the abdominal region (Fig. 5(b), Fig. S12). The experimental results demonstrate that FS-CDs exhibit excellent and stable fluorescence characteristics, suggesting their potential utility as a bioimaging probe.

In conclusion, a novel solvothermal method for the production of FS-CDs has been developed. The well-functionalized surface of FS-CDs produced visible PL with strong excitation-dependent emission at 510 nm and a superior PLQY of 31.71%, which is higher than previously reported values. Based on the XPS and fluorescence emission spectra analysis, the high PLQY of FS-CDs could be attributed to the (N, O)-codoped and high nitrogen doping. Additionally, fluorescent bioimaging of zebrafish was performed utilizing the FS-CDs sample as a probe. The findings confirmed the efficacy of FS-CDs as a probe in the field of bioimaging applications. This discovery not only implies the high value-added property of fish scale and expands its application, but it also provides a green, cost-effective, and large-scale commercial strategy for the production of CDs derived from biomass.

Yao Liu: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Yaoyao Tang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Quan Xu: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yi Cao: Formal analysis. Jianfei Wei: Methodology. Zhiqiang Liang: Methodology. Xinyi Jiang: Methodology.Ying Wu: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Chaozhong Guo: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (KJQN202001335 and KJQN202101313), Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQJQX0032), National Defense Science and Technology Excellence Youth Fund 2022-JCJQ-ZQ-018.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2024.120583.
